diagrams.title: Image Degradation and Restoration Process Model
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing the complete degradation and restoration pipeline with periodic noise as the degradation source
elements: Original Image f(x,y) - The clean input image
Degradation Function H - The system that introduces blur/distortion
Additive Noise η(x,y) - Including periodic noise components
Degraded Image g(x,y) - The corrupted output
Frequency Domain Analysis - Transform to identify noise frequencies
Restoration Filter - Design based on noise characteristics
Restored Image f̂(x,y) - The final output after filtering
diagrams.purpose: Shows the complete pipeline of how images get degraded by periodic noise and restored using frequency domain filtering. Students understand where periodic noise enters and how filtering removes it.

diagrams.title: Periodic Noise Spatial Domain vs Frequency Domain Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side visualization showing periodic noise pattern in spatial domain and its distinct representation in frequency domain
elements: Left side: Spatial domain image with wavy/striped patterns representing periodic noise
Right side: Frequency spectrum (FFT) showing bright impulse pairs at specific frequencies
Center: DC component (bright spot at origin)
Impulse pairs: Symmetric bright spots appearing at noise frequencies (u0,v0) and (-u0,-v0)
Frequency axes: u (horizontal) and v (vertical) with origin at center
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates how periodic noise appears as distinct impulse peaks in the frequency domain, making it easy to identify and filter out. This is the key insight for understanding why frequency domain filtering works.

diagrams.title: Notch Rejection Filter Frequency Response
diagrams.description: 3D surface plot and 2D cross-section of a typical notch rejection filter used for periodic noise removal
elements: 3D Surface: Shows rejection bands at specific frequencies (peaks = pass, valleys = stop)
2D Top view: Circular or rectangular stop regions at noise frequency locations
Pass region: Most of the frequency spectrum remains unchanged
Stop region: Deep notch at the noise frequency (u0,v0) and symmetric (-u0,-v0)
Filter transfer function H(u,v) ranging from 0 (stop) to 1 (pass)
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes how notch rejection filters selectively block only the specific frequencies where periodic noise exists while preserving the rest of the image content.

diagrams.title: Periodic Noise Reduction Process Flowchart
diagrams.description: Step-by-step algorithmic flowchart for reducing periodic noise using frequency domain filtering
elements: Step 1: Input Degraded Image g(x,y)
Step 2: Compute 2D DFT → G(u,v)
Step 3: Display frequency spectrum and identify noise peaks (bright spots)
Step 4: Determine noise frequency coordinates (u0,v0)
Step 5: Design Notch Rejection Filter H(u,v) at identified frequencies
Step 6: Multiply G(u,v) × H(u,v) → F(u,v)
Step 7: Compute Inverse DFT
Step 8: Output Restored Image f̂(x,y) with real part extracted
diagrams.purpose: Provides a clear algorithmic roadmap for students to implement periodic noise reduction, showing the exact sequence of operations in the frequency domain approach.

diagrams.title: Ideal vs Butterworth Band-Stop Filter Comparison
diagrams.description: Comparison of two common band-stop filter profiles used for periodic noise reduction
elements: Ideal Band-Stop: Sharp cutoff, rectangular stopband, abrupt transition
Butterworth Band-Stop: Smooth transition, no ringing artifacts, order-based rolloff
Frequency axes showing passband and stopband regions
Stopband centered at noise frequency with bandwidth W
Cutoff frequencies: ωc1 and ωc2 defining the stopband
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand the trade-offs between ideal (sharp but ringing) and practical (smooth) filters for periodic noise removal, important for implementation decisions.

charts.title: Frequency Domain Filters for Periodic Noise Reduction
charts.content: Comparison of filter types: Ideal Band-Stop (sharp cutoff but ringing), Butterworth Band-Stop (smooth, configurable order), Gaussian Band-Stop (smoothest, no ringing), Notch Reject (removes specific frequencies only). Columns: Filter Type, Frequency Response, Advantages, Disadvantages, Best Use Case.

charts.title: Steps in Periodic Noise Reduction Algorithm
charts.content: Linear sequential steps: (1) Load degraded image → (2) Apply FFT/DFT → (3) Shift zero frequency to center → (4) Analyze spectrum for impulse peaks → (5) Record noise frequency coordinates → (6) Design appropriate filter → (7) Apply filter in frequency domain → (8) Apply inverse shift → (9) Apply inverse DFT → (10) Extract real component and normalize → (11) Display restored image.

charts.title: Types of Periodic Noise Based on Frequency Characteristics
charts.content: Classification hierarchy: Single-frequency periodic noise (simple sine wave, creates 2 impulse peaks), Multi-frequency periodic noise (multiple sine components, multiple impulse pairs), Horizontal/Vertical noise (impulses on axes), Grid noise (impulses in grid pattern). Each type shows typical frequency domain appearance.

charts.title: Noise Identification in Frequency Domain
charts.content: Identification guide: Gaussian noise (broad spectrum, no distinct peaks), Periodic noise (distinct bright impulse pairs at specific frequencies), Salt-and-pepper noise (broad spectrum with impulses throughout), Speckle noise (multiplicative, affects all frequencies). Columns: Noise Type, Spatial Appearance, Frequency Domain Appearance, Filtering Strategy.

mindMaps.centralConcept: Periodic Noise Reduction by Frequency Domain Filtering
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting theoretical concepts to practical implementation. Shows students how periodic noise properties (regular, repetitive patterns) map to distinct frequency components, which filters can target those frequencies, and where this technique is applied in real-world scenarios like medical imaging and document scanning.

mindMaps.centralConcept: Frequency Domain Filtering for Image Restoration
mindMaps.purpose: Places periodic noise reduction within the broader context of image restoration, showing students how this specific technique connects to other restoration methods and quality assessment approaches they might encounter in the course.
